summary: I was born in 1933 in Rye, New York, into a Jewish family with a complex background. My early life was marked by family tensions, but I found transformation and inspiration through political activism, music, and my experiences at an interracial summer camp in upstate New York. I went on to study social science at the University of Chicago, where I engaged deeply with leftist politics and folk music, eventually leading the Folklore Society and organizing concerts featuring artists like Pete Seeger and Big Bill Broonzy.

I was born in Rye, New York, in Westchester County in 1933. My family lived there during the Great Depression, and my father ran a grocery store that eventually went bankrupt. Rye was the place where my early family life unfolded, including a memorable moment when Amelia Earhart visited our store and held me as a newborn.

A remarkable moment from my infancy was when Amelia Earhart came into my father's grocery store in Rye, New York, to buy groceries. I was just a newborn then, and she picked me up and held me. I've often thought about that encounter as a special connection, even if it was just a brief and casual moment.

My father was an immigrant from Eastern Europe who came to the United States around the time of the First World War, though I can't recall exactly if it was before or after the war. This timing affected my family's history and dynamics, especially given the immigrant and non-immigrant tensions within our household. The war period indirectly shaped the environment in which I was raised.

My uncle Paul was a union organizer for the International Ladies' Garment Workers' Union, which connected me to labor activism from a young age. This family involvement in unions influenced my political awareness and commitment to leftist causes. The ILGWU was part of the broader socialist and labor movements that shaped my upbringing and activism.

My uncle Joe grew up in Vienna, where he was exposed to a vibrant socialist political environment. His European background and political engagement deeply influenced me, especially as he supported my education and political awakening. Vienna was a key part of his identity and shaped his leftist views that he passed on to me.

In 1948, during Henry A. Wallace's presidential campaign, I attended an interracial summer camp in upstate New York that was connected to the progressive and leftist politics of the time. Although the counselors were not allowed to wear Wallace for President buttons, the political atmosphere was palpable and transformative for me. Wallace's campaign was part of the broader context that shaped my early political consciousness.

The 1948 presidential election was a significant backdrop to my experiences at the interracial summer camp in Roscoe, New York. It was the year Henry A. Wallace ran for president, and the political tensions and activism of that election influenced the camp's atmosphere and my own political development. Although the counselors couldn't openly display their support, the election energized the leftist community I was part of.

Willowemoc was the interracial summer camp I attended in Roscoe, New York, starting when I was 13. It was run by Dr. Thomas, an African American doctor, and was a place where I experienced a transformative mix of music, leftist politics, and racial integration. I went there for several years and even became a junior counselor, and the camp profoundly shaped my views on race and social justice.

Roscoe, New York, is the small town where the Willowemoc summer camp was located. I traveled there from Washington Heights, Manhattan, to attend this interracial camp during the late 1940s. Roscoe became a place of personal growth and political awakening for me, far from the tensions of my family life in Rye.

I remember taking the bus from Washington Heights, Manhattan, to the Willowemoc camp in Roscoe, New York. It was a significant departure point for me to escape my difficult family environment and enter a new world of political and social awakening. The journey from Washington Heights marked the beginning of a transformative chapter in my life.

Larry Pallas was a folksinger and square dance caller I met at the Willowemoc camp. He even came to my 16th birthday party, which was a square dancing event, and his music was part of the folk traditions that deeply influenced me. Larry was one of the many leftist musicians who shaped my love for folk music and political activism.

I attended the University of Chicago for college, studying social science and engaging in both politics and art. The university had the Hutchins Program, which was based on the Great Books, and I thrived in that intellectually rigorous environment. I also became head of the Folklore Society there, organizing concerts with folk musicians like Pete Seeger and Big Bill Broonzy, blending my academic and musical interests.

Robert M. Hutchins was the educator behind the special program at the University of Chicago that I was part of. His Hutchins Program emphasized studying the Great Books, which shaped my academic experience profoundly. This program combined with my political and musical interests made my college years transformative.

The Great Books were central to my education at the University of Chicago through the Hutchins Program. Studying these classic texts gave me a strong foundation in social science, history, and philosophy, complementing my political activism and musical pursuits. The Great Books curriculum was a key part of my intellectual development during college.

I served as head of the Folklore Society at the University of Chicago, where I organized concerts and events featuring prominent folk musicians like Pete Seeger and Big Bill Broonzy. This role allowed me to merge my passion for music and leftist politics, fostering a vibrant community around folk traditions and activism. The society was a significant part of my college experience.

Pete Seeger was one of the folk musicians I helped bring to the University of Chicago as head of the Folklore Society. His music and activism inspired me deeply and connected with the leftist political environment I was part of. Seeing him perform was a highlight of my college years and my involvement in folk music.

Big Bill Broonzy was another influential folk musician I organized to perform at the University of Chicago during my time leading the Folklore Society. His presence enriched the folk music scene I was passionate about and linked music with social and political activism. His concerts were memorable events that shaped my cultural and political interests.
